titleStmt.title: Saudi Arabia 2022 – Speaker 14 – Education (Girls) (Part 1)
WIBARAB Corpus
titleStmt.funder: Funded by the European Research Council under the Grant Agreement 101020127

publicationStmt.distributor: Austrian Center for Digital Humanities and Cultural Heritage

recording.p: Recorded during Saudi Arabia Fieldwork Campaign Gunda Kinzl 2022

prefixDef.p: Private URIs using the teiCorpusHeader prefix are pointers to any element in the WIBARAB teiCorpus document.
Private URIs using the share prefix are pointers to audio file residing on the WIBARAB network share.
Private URIs using the geo prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a place element in VICAV Geodata list.
Private URIs using the dmp prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB DMP document..
Private URIs using the zotid prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a biblStruct element in the TEI export of the VICAV Zotero Group library.
Private URIs using the sources prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB list of sources.

spanGrp.span: Girls from the city, from the Ḥarb tribe and the Muḥammadi tribe and the Ḥarb, from the tribes
and here the dialect started to change
very well, simultaniously with the schools
simultaniously with the schools. How did this generation change? With the schools.

For us it was done, we absorbed our dialect and that was that, but they were not raised (with this dialect). At a young age and that's it, they wanted to change their dialects, they took it as if it was tradition from the beginning.
It was like this, not just for the Rašāyda but for all the tribes it was like this.
and what? They took it (this new dialect) from the teachers, I am talking here about the girls, I don't know how it was with the boys
The girls, I noticed them, I noticed it in the generation of my sisters who came after me, I was surprised that their speech was different, not like ours.

Even their friends, if you had talked with their friends, (you would notice that) their dialect has changed, they adopted the dialect of the other cities, the people in the city and others as such, they copied it until it became like so, and that's it.
Do you see? and that it how education was here, afterwards no, this was over, everyone started to educate their daughters, in the city a dorm for female students was opened, for education.
Understood.
Yes, and that was it. The people would enroll their daughters in the department and in the dorm
On Thursdays and Fridays there weren't any classes so they would go and bring them back to their families for two days and then they would return back, and it was like that.

And that's it, most of them here, it would be strange if someone wouldn't have graduated, most of them (women) here have graduated, but additionally most of them did not get a position, because there weren't any positions, meaning they did not become employed, there weren't any jobs.
In the last couple of years there haven't been any jobs, meaning...
For the woman, or for everyone?
No, regarding teaching (positions).
There hasn't been the demand for teaching positions. Some specialized subjects have died out, for some specialized subjects there isn't any demand, you know what demand is, right?
Here, the ministry of education doesn't see the need.
These are its directions. What else?
for example, had I not got a post or graduated, and stayed at home for a year
After that they opened the schools for the erasure of illiteracy, do you know about them?
(Schools) for the erasure of illiteracy, do you know what erasure of illiteracy means?
For educating of the older women
Yes
Yes, they opened them in abundance in the area, thank God.
In Xaybar, in Ṯimid, in Ṣōṣla, in Ləhən, where you have already been.
Because the Bedouins were a lot in number, and many came, whose daughters were not educated.
There were illiterate, meaning they didn't know anything, my school was here in Dība, in Dība.
Girls used to come to us not long ago, 'dūb-hum', 'dūb-hum' meaning recently.
'dūb-hum' I tell you 'dūb-ha', 'dūb-ha' meaning recently, a short while ago 'dūb-a'
(for example) 'where were you? I was here dūb-i' meaning where I was here a short while ago, or I can say taww-i
or I can say taww-i, taww-i meaning that I was here since a short period
A short while ago for them, meaning the girls, the bedouins, they were living them, (like) my uncle, my uncle the brother of my father
possibly from ʕāṣim, she had said? From ʕāṣim, sixteen, eighteen years ago
Now he has been settled here for eighteen years, before he was a nomad, he was a nomad and he and his daughters and his sons were not educated
They were living in al-Qasīm, in the desert of al-Gasīm, they have areas there, they were living in tents

the sheep, every aspect of their life revolved around the sheep, they sold them and their work was dependent on them and they would take all of their needs, they would buy them off the sheep (from herding).
As for the water, they naturally had big water bowsers, big containers for water
They would go and come back, they say 'nirid', 'nirid' meaning we go to a place which has water, a well, and they load the water
To load, do you know what 'iʕabbi' means? He loads the bowsers, meaning he carries water

*Addresses another woman* Go, check on the macaroni, ʕAmal go and put on the gas from above, you have (two settings) from above and from under, leave only (one heat setting) from above, so that is reddened
Even their daughters did not learn, when they came her they enrolled into the schools for the erasure of illiteracy, they used to come to us ...
But this was late
Yes, of course. In the schools for the erasure of literature they could be enrolled from the age of ten upwards.
We had a lot of girls who were learning
And 'barḏu' (additionally), you see 'barḏu' is not a word typically used by the Rašāyda
It seems like it is possibly Egyptian
Yes, there are mixtures, it made its way us, blessings on the Prophet Muḥammad
The country had a role in the settling of the Bedouin
It even ... do you know about social security? Security.
Yes I know about it.
Which is what?
Like an insurance.

Yes, for those who did not have an income, who did not have a salary, who was poor, and things like that, they would give him, he would put forward his papers and (show that) he has children and they would give him a sum
The country had decided that they need to ... they would not be able to claim the social security money unless their daughters were enrolled in schools.
And so this had a powerful impact, it was a powerful incentive for them to send their daughters to school.

They used to, indeed they used to bring their daughters, even if they lived far away they would bring their daughters, even if originally they still, they were not living in houses, meaning they lived quite far away, they used to travel and bring their daughters to us so that they could study.
For the reason of (acquiring) social security, it worked, it worked (in favour) of education and it worked (in favour) of women.
